mustSees.text: Quick, accurate recognition and appropriate assessment of situation - signal for help
Effective communication and response with other rescuer
Lowest risk rescue possible under the circumstances and concern for personal safety throughout
Appropriate choice and use of aid(s)
Safe and effective entry, approach (maintaining visual contact), reverse and ready
Effective tow or carry (when used)
Victim secured at nearest point of safety
Effective use of bystanders where appropriate
Safe and effective removal with bystander assistance
Contact with EMS at earliest possible moment if appropriate
Victim Assessment (ABCs) and appropriate care until rescuers relieved of responsibility
Effective use of barrier devices if required
Evaluation of the rescue scene
Removal of/from danger
Appropriate communication with victim
Assessment of level of consciousness
Assessment of airway, breathing, circulation
Relevant history (accident, medical, personal)
Taking and recording vital signs (respiration, pulse, pupils, skin, LOC)
Systematic head-to-toe examination of victim
Assessment of environment for hazards
Establish Unresponsiveness
Activate EMS
Attempt to obtain AED and recruit AED-trained responder
Open airway: head-tilt/chin-lift
Visual check for breathing (5 sec.)
CPR started with 30 chest compressions (or with 2 rescue breaths for drowning victims)
If victim begins to move, reassess ABCs and treat appropriately
AED usage
Assessment of degree of obstruction - ask 'Are you choking?'
Rescuer identifies self - 'Can I help?'
Mild - Coughing encouraged
Mild - Reassurance for victim
Severe - Shout for help
Severe - Careful landmarking
Severe - Alternating back blows/abdominal thrusts until airway is clear (chest thrusts for pregnant/obese victim)
Severe - If successful, direct victim to see a physician to rule out complications from the obstruction or the abdominal thrusts.
Infant - 5 back blows and 5 chest thrusts
Infant - Back blows and thrusts until effective or the infant becomes unconscious
Infant - If successful, caregiver directed to take victim to see a physician to rule out complications from the obstruction or the treatement
Assessment of environment for hazards
Establish Unresponsiveness
Activate EMS
Attempt to obtain AED and recruit AED-trained responder
Open airway: head-tilt/chin-lift
Visual check for breathing (5 sec.)
If breathing is absent or abnormal, CPR started with 30 chest compressions (or with 2 rescue breaths for drowning victims)
Attempt to ventilate
If ventilation successful - Continue CPR sequence
If ventilation unsuccessful - Resposition airway and re-attempt to ventilate
Careful landmarking and 30 chest compressions
Foreign body check: look in mouth and if object can be seen, attempt to remove it
Attempt to ventilate. Repeat sequence (reposition head, re-attempt to ventilate, chest compressions, foreign body check) until successful
Determination of cause (and removal if possible) of emergency
Victim and scene assessment
EMS activated if necessary
Individual placed in most comfortable position
Victim reassured
Airway and respiration maintained
Asthma - Victim assisted with medication (inhaler)
Encouragement of pursed lip breathing
Victim and scene assessment
EMS Activated
Victim reassured
Victim placed in position of comfort, preferably recovery position
Victim kept warm
Vital signs monitored
Victim and scene assessment
EMS Activated
Rescuer has victim stop activity and sit or lay in a position most comfortable for the victim
Victim reassured
Vital signs monitored
Medical history requested (e.g. cardiovascular disease)
Victim helped to take medication (nitroglycerine) if available; rescuer does not administer
Victim and scene assessment
EMS activated if necessary
Application of dressing or bandage
No aggravation of injury
Distal circulation check
Direct pressure over wound or around wound if contains impaled object
Reassurance for victim and instruction to rest
Victim and scene assessment
EMS activated
Victim placed in recovery position or position of comfort
Vital signs monitored
Victim reassured
Victim and scene assessment
EMS activated
Victim reassured
Victim placed in a comfortable position with as little movement as possible until help arrives
External bleeding (if present) controlled and wounds dressed
Victim and scene assessment
EMS activated if necessary
Victim removed from source of injury
Burn site immediately cooled with cool water
Injury covered with dry clean (preferably sterile) dressings
Wet Chemical - Flushed with lots of water
Dry Chemical - Dust before flushing with water
Check for entry and exit burns, immobilize affected limbs
Victim and scene assessment
EMS activated if necessary
Determination of (and if possible, removal from) cause of injury
Selected treatment appropriate for the injury
Victim and scene assessment
EMS activated if necessary
Victim placed in recovery position (except if suspected spinal injury or if moving victim will aggravate injuries)
Airway maintained
Rescuer looks for reason for unconscousness (e.g. Medic Alert tag, syringe, environmental factors such as heat)
Rescuer #1 - Assessment of environment for hazards
Rescuer #1 - One-rescuer CPR sequence
Rescuer #2 - Identifies self as CPR trained & confirms EMS activation
Both - Continue CPR and switch roles with as little interruption as possible
Both - CPR continued until EMS takes over, or an AED-trained responder begins treatment with an AED unit, or the victim begins to move
Both - If the victim begins to move, ABCs reassessed and appropriate treatment
AED - Communication and coordination between rescuers throughout the AED protocol
AED - AED applied; clothing removed (chest bare), skin prepared, appropriate positioning of electrodes, and connection to defibrillator
AED - Appropriate response to voice prompts and machine indicators
AED - Victim cleared to for analysis ensuring no motion or contact with others; visual check and 'all clear' stated for analysis and shock
AED - AED prompts followed (sequence of analyze/shock, followed immedidately by about 2 minutes of CPR) until EMS takes over treatment, or victim begins to move
Quick, accurate recognition
Victim and scene assessment
EMS Activated
Spinal movement restricted throughout: Conscious victim reassured and directed not to move
Appropriate care of victim throughout
Victim and scene assessment
Victim reassured
Determination of cause of injury and removal from cause
EMS activated if necessary
Heat - Victim cooled and attempt to reduce core temperature
Dehydration - Attempt to provide fluids - preferably electrolyte drink with carbohydration (for dehydration due to exertion)
Cold - Attempt to increase core temperature
Frostbite - Protection from further injury - apply dressing and keep warm
Victim and scene assessment
EMS activated if necessary
Immobilization of injury site
Check for sensation and circulation above and below the injury
Treatment of wounds associated with the injury
If available, ice applied to injury site
Victim and scene assessment
EMS Activated
Victim reassured
Victim placed in a comfortable position with as little movement as possible until help arrives
External bleeding (if present) controlled and wounds dressed
Victim and scene assessment
EMS activated if necessary
Spinal motion restricted if spinal injury suspected
Bandaging if situation requires (i.e. if no bleeding/discharge, no bandage required)
Vital signs monitored
Treatment with injured side down
Victim and scene assessment
EMS activated if necessary
Recognition of seizure
Protection of head
Rescuer does not restrain or immobilize victim or put anything into the victim's mouth
Removal of dangers to victim
Victim placed in recovery psoition after seizure has stopped
Victim and scene assessment
EMS activated if victim is unconscious or if conscious victim does not improve
Recognition of situation through symptoms and medical history
Sugar administered if victim is conscious
Victim and scene assessment
EMS activated if necessary
Determination of cause of poisoning: identification and collection of sample (and container) of substance if feasible
Avoidance of exposure to the poison
Treatment - Naloxone / Epipen / Other
Poison Control Center or EMS contacted for treatment information
Vital signs monitored
Appropriate use of verbal and non-verbal communication skills (language, tone, facial expressions, body language)
Ability to instill and model trust through clear and open communication
Ability to educate patrons about safe practices
Ability to deal positively and fairly with patrons
Recognition of conflict and the need for safety-centered resolution
Appropriate use of communication techniques: hand signals
Appropriate use of communication techniques: signalling devices
Ability to give and receive directions
Appropriate use of communication techniques: hand signals
Immediate and accurate communication with others in an emergency
Clear and concise communcation with EMS at earliest possible moment under the circumstances
Appropriate use of verbal and non-verbal communication skills (language, tone, facial expressions, body language)
Ability to instill and model trust through clear and open communication
Ability to educate patrons about safe practices
Ability to deal positively and fairly with patrons
Recognition of conflict and the need for safety-centered resolution
Appropriate use of communication techniques: hand signals
Vigilant, attentive and alert demeanor
Systematic scanning pattern with observable head movement
Continuous scan of designated supervision zone including bottom
Quick, accurate recognition and analysis of potential incidenets and of patrons needing assistance
Recognition of communication from other lifeguards (e.g. hand and whistle signals)
Immediate search of water area and the bottom of all pools
Complete description of missing person obtained (name, age, size, clothing, etc.) with other key information such as where last seen
Lifeguard remains with the reporting party
Accurate and timely relay of missing person description to other lifeguards and bystanders
A systematic, thorough search of the facility starting in the area where missing person was last seen or the area of greatest danger if not seen
Appropriate and effective direction of bystanders where applicable
Good teamwork: back-up, follow-up, crowd control
Reassurance, assistance, and direction to further authority for the person reporting the incident
Quick recognition and immediate response
Safe and appropriate entry with or without aid
Quick, efficient approach
Motion of victim's spine restricted during roll-over (if required) and throughout
Victim's airway supported above surface
Safe and effective removal with assistance
Effective communication with other lifeguards, victim, patrons, bystanders
Effective use of barrier devices (e.g. pocket mask, gloves)
ABC assessment - Stabilize breathing victim
ABC assessment - Immediately removal & CPR non-breathing victim
EMS activated at earliest possible moment
ABC reassessment and ability to deal with complications (e.g. airway obstruction, vomiting, gastric distension)
Secondary assessment
Effective lifeguard positioning
Effective observation skills and scanning techniques
Timely recognition and appropriate response
Leadership in the management of the situation
Effective communication with auxiliary staff, victim, patrons, bystanders
Appropriate selection and effective demonstration of rescue techniques and use of equipment
Effective use of barrier devices (e.g. pocket mask, gloves)
Appropriate first aid treatment if required
Accurate communication with EMS (if appropriate) at earliest possible moment
Continued safety supervision of other patrons throughout or water cleared
Concern for good public relations
Accurate reporting and appropriate follow-up
Effective lifeguard positioning
Effective observation skills and scanning techniques
Timely recognition and appropriate response
Effective performance as a team leader and as a team member in the management of the situation
Effective communication with other lifeguards, assistant lifeguards, victims, patrons, bystanders, and auxiliary staff
Appropriate selection and effective demonstration of rescue techniques and use of equipment
Effective use of barrier devices (e.g. pocket mask, gloves)
Appropriate first aid treatment if required
Accurate communication with EMS (if appropriate) at earliest possible moment
Continued safety supervision of other patrons throughout or water cleared
Concern for good public relations
Accurate reporting and appropriate follow-up
